role_summary: Dentist; adult adviser to the NAACP Youth Council in St. Augustine, Florida; president of the SCLC Florida branch; survivor of a September 1963 KKK kidnapping and beating; invited Martin Luther King Jr. to St. Augustine in spring 1964, the campaign that helped produce the Civil Rights Act of 1964.
ai_reading: Hayling's top semantic neighbor in this corpus is C. T. Vivian (entry 134) at a cosine count of 220, nearly double his next neighbor, the joint Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer interview (entry 4, count 115). The Vivian pairing tracks the cultural record (Hayling led the St. Augustine campaign that drew King and SCLC staff, and he names Vivian in his own testimony as one of the Nashville movement figures he came to know in dental school), yet the count runs unusually high for an interviewee whose public placement is regional and dental-professional rather than SCLC-headquarters. The next neighbors are Robert L. Carter (entry 108, count 114), Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan (entry 51, count 112), and H. Jack Geiger (entry 54, count 104). His concept-axis positions are tightly centrist: four of the five normalized positions fall within +/-0.11 of zero (nonviolence-self-defense at 0.005 and sacred-secular at 0.006 sit almost exactly at the midpoint), and only the Southern Struggle vs Northern Struggle axis reaches 0.18 toward the Southern pole. That clustering matches the near-zero vector pattern visible in Charles McDew (entry 16) and Gloria Hayes Richardson (entry 49), and it is the novel reading here: for a survivor of a documented 1963 Klan kidnapping and beating, the embedding does not pull his testimony toward any pole, possibly catching the dentist's clinical-professional register more than the survivor's pain-narrative register. The one influence-graph edge that names Hayling runs from Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan (entry 51), who is also among his closest semantic neighbors, so the youth-cohort tie shows up in both the discussed-by graph and the cosine space.
biographical_paragraph: Hayling is recorded as the first African American dentist admitted to the previously all-white St. Johns County and Florida state components of the American Dental Association, and the secondary record describes him as the first Black dentist elected to its local, regional, and national components [src: 2]. As adult adviser to the NAACP Youth Council in St. Augustine he guided sit-ins and picketing through 1963 and 1964, objecting that no Black resident had been placed on the committees planning the federally funded quadricentennial of the city's 1565 founding [src: 2][src: 7]. In September 1963 he and three companions (James Jackson, Clyde Jenkins, and James Hauser) were seized at a Ku Klux Klan rally outside the city and beaten; Florida Highway Patrol officers, alerted by an Episcopal priest who had infiltrated the rally, intervened before the men were set afire [src: 5][src: 4]. He affiliated with the Florida leadership of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference and in spring 1964 invited Martin Luther King Jr. and SCLC staff to St. Augustine; the campaign that followed was a precipitating event for the passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 [src: 2][src: 7]. He was inducted into the Florida Civil Rights Hall of Fame in 2014 alongside A. Philip Randolph and James Weldon Johnson; St. Augustine's largest park was later named in his honor [src: 2]. Born November 20, 1929 in Tallahassee, Florida, Hayling died December 20, 2015 at his home in Fort Lauderdale at the age of eighty-six [src: 5][src: 10]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 103 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Robert Bagner Hayling
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the grievance that drove the early St. Augustine protests, the all-white committees planning the federally funded 400th-anniversary celebration:
interview_snippets.quote: Not one black, even though at the time the black population was somewhere close to 20% of the city had been appointed to any of the committees involved with the complete year of celebrating the 400th anniversary.

interview_snippets.speaker: Robert Bagner Hayling
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked to describe the injuries from the September 1963 Klan beating:
interview_snippets.quote: Well I usually summarize by saying that the injuries I encountered I would take to my grief.

interview_snippets.speaker: Robert Bagner Hayling
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the beating's deliberate damage to the right arm he depended on as a dentist:
interview_snippets.quote: If I had not been in certain physical condition to rehabilitate the muscles and the ligaments in this arm I would have had to quit my practice.

interview_snippets.speaker: Robert Bagner Hayling
interview_snippets.lead_in: On returning the local charter after Roy Wilkins threatened to revoke it, breaking St. Augustine from the national NAACP:
interview_snippets.quote: In other words in the new telling me we're fired I'm saying we resign and I packaged up through Mrs. Elizabeth Hawthorne and all and sent a mail special delivery the charter with a return receipt to New York to let them know that we were going to go on with what we were doing.

interview_snippets.speaker: Robert Bagner Hayling
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the St. Augustine campaign's refusal to halt demonstrations during the 1964 legislative fight:
interview_snippets.quote: We said we were not going to stop demonstrating until the Civil Rights Act of 1964 had been signed.

interview_snippets.speaker: Barbara Edna Vickers
interview_snippets.lead_in: Hayling's across-the-street neighbor Barbara Edna Vickers describes the armed community watch that guarded his home at night:
interview_snippets.quote: But the West Side Boys came and they were in the trees and all around Dr. Robert B. Hayling House to protect him.

interview_snippets.speaker: Barbara Edna Vickers
interview_snippets.lead_in: Vickers recalls that King declined Hayling's first request before agreeing to come to St. Augustine:
interview_snippets.quote: Dr. Robert B. Hayling went and asked Dr. King, he turned him down the first time he wouldn't come. But when he finally got him here, and that night he asked me about would I participate in the churches?

interview_snippets.speaker: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
interview_snippets.lead_in: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan, who came up in St. Augustine and is the one interviewee the influence graph records as discussing Hayling, names him among those she credits:
interview_snippets.quote: But I think the Lord for the Twines, I think the Lord for Dr. Robert B. Hayling, I learned about him later in life as I grew up.

movement_context: Hayling moved to St. Augustine in 1960 to open a dental practice on Bridge Street; within months he had taken on the role of adult adviser to the local NAACP Youth Council, a position he held through the central organizing years of 1960 to 1964 [src: 3][src: 4]. The Youth Council under his guidance ran sit-ins at the segregated lunch counters of St. Augustine's downtown commercial corridor, organized picketing of the Ponce de Leon Hotel and the Plaza de la Constitucion, and confronted the city's preparations for the federally funded 1965 quadricentennial celebration; Hayling argued that no federal money should subsidize a celebration that excluded the city's Black residents [src: 3][src: 5]. In September 1963 Hayling, Clyde Jenkins, James Hauser, and James Jackson drove out to observe a Ku Klux Klan rally on the outskirts of St. Augustine from a parked car on U.S. 1; they were surrounded, ordered out, and beaten near-unconscious with ax handles, baseball bats, and chains, then piled on the speaker's stage [src: 5]. In his oral history Hayling recounts that the rally's announcer stated an intent to douse the four with gasoline and set them afire, and that the beating left him having lost eleven teeth, with deliberate damage to the right arm he relied on as a dentist [src: 1]. Florida Highway Patrol officers, summoned by an Episcopal priest from Daytona Beach who had infiltrated the rally with a concealed recorder, reached the field before the threatened burning [src: 1][src: 5]. The four men's beating drew national press coverage and is documented in Colburn's Racial Change and Community Crisis and Branch's Pillar of Fire as a precipitating event that pulled SCLC into the city [src: 3][src: 4]. In early 1964 Hayling, then serving as president of Florida SCLC, issued a formal invitation to Martin Luther King Jr. and SCLC staff to make St. Augustine the spring 1964 campaign target; King, Andrew Young, and Hosea Williams arrived in late May, opening a sustained six-week direct-action phase that included night marches into Plaza de la Constitucion, kneel-ins at segregated white churches (the same tactical pattern St. Augustine organizer Barbara Edna Vickers, catalog entry 5, participated in), and the wade-ins at St. Augustine Beach and the Monson Motor Lodge pool [src: 4][src: 5]. On June 11, 1964 King and Ralph Abernathy were arrested on the steps of the segregated Monson Motor Lodge restaurant on the St. Augustine bayfront, the only arrest of King's career inside Florida [src: 4][src: 7]. On June 18, 1964 Monson manager James Brock poured muriatic acid into the motel pool while integrated demonstrators were swimming together, the photograph (carried by the Associated Press and the Tampa Tribune) widely credited in the historiographical record with shifting Senate Republican Minority Leader Everett Dirksen's stance on cloture [src: 3][src: 4]. The cloture vote ending the seventy-five-day filibuster against the civil rights bill was held the following day; the bill cleared the Senate June 19 and was signed into law by President Johnson on July 2, 1964 [src: 4]. The youth foot-soldier cohort Hayling had assembled through the NAACP Youth Council included teenage participants like Purcell Maurice Conway (catalog entry 99) and Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan (catalog entry 51), whose later oral histories ground the St. Augustine campaign's youth-base structural pattern [src: 6][src: 8].
legacy_and_reception: Hayling relocated his dental practice to Cocoa, Florida, near Cape Kennedy, after completing the five-year service obligation that had brought him to St. Augustine; the secondary record adds that his Bridge Street office had been firebombed and his liability insurance canceled [src: 5][src: 6]. He practiced in Cocoa for the remainder of his career [src: 5]. The St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument, dedicated May 14, 2011 in the Plaza de la Constitucion, lists Hayling among the thirty-one foot soldiers named on its central plaque alongside the Youth Council and adult organizers he had recruited and led [src: 5][src: 9]. The ACCORD Civil Rights Museum, opened in St. Augustine in 2014, occupies the restored Bridge Street building that housed Hayling's original dental office; the museum's permanent exhibits center the September 1963 Klan abduction, the 1964 SCLC campaign, and the Youth Council's organizing infrastructure [src: 5]. Hayling was inducted into the Florida Civil Rights Hall of Fame in 2014; in 2015 the city of St. Augustine renamed its largest municipal park as Dr. Robert B. Hayling Freedom Park in his honor [src: 2][src: 5]. He died December 20, 2015 at his home in Fort Lauderdale at the age of eighty-six [src: 5][src: 10]. His Library of Congress oral history, recorded September 14, 2011 by interviewer Joseph Mosnier as part of the Civil Rights History Project's Florida fieldwork, is catalogued at the American Folklife Center as accession AFC 2010/039 and at the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture as accession 2011.174.46 [src: 1].

gallery.caption: St. Augustine civil rights demonstrations, 1964, from the Florida Memory project of the State Library and Archives of Florida. The footage documents demonstrators at Butler Beach, integrated occupation of city streets, and intervention by the Florida Highway Patrol during the spring 1964 SCLC direct-action campaign Hayling organized as president of Florida SCLC.
gallery.photographer: State Library and Archives of Florida (Florida Memory project)

gallery.alt: James Brock pouring muriatic acid into the Monson Motor Lodge swimming pool, June 18, 1964
gallery.caption: Monson Motor Lodge manager James Brock pouring muriatic acid into the motel's swimming pool during the June 18, 1964 swim-in by integrated demonstrators, photographed by Vernon Barchard for the Tampa Tribune. The image, widely reproduced by the Associated Press, is the most-cited single photograph from the St. Augustine campaign Hayling and Florida SCLC had organized, and is credited in the historiographical record with shifting Senate Republican Minority Leader Everett Dirksen's cloture position on the civil rights bill.
gallery.license: Public domain (US, published 1931-1977 without copyright notice)

gallery.alt: St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument plaque, dedicated May 14, 2011
gallery.caption: The St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument plaque in the Plaza de la Constitucion, dedicated May 14, 2011. Hayling is among the thirty-one foot soldiers' names inscribed on the central plaque, alongside the Youth Council and adult organizers he led through the 1960-1964 St. Augustine campaign.
